---
title: "Four Popular MCDM Methods: TOPSIS, VIKOR, PROMETHEE, and ELECTRE"
author: " "
date: today
format: 
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: "/Users/nam-anhtran/heartofsaigon/styles/styles_doc.css"
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    self-contained-math: true
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: katex
    number-sections: true
    include-in-header: "/Users/nam-anhtran/heartofsaigon/styles/noindex.html"
execute: 
  echo: false
  eval: false
  fig-height: 7
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/sage-vancouver-brackets
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

:::callout-title
[heartofsaigon/published_docs/03]
:::

## Overview

Multi-criteria decision making (MCDM) is used when we must choose among options (called **alternatives**) while considering multiple criteria at the same time (for example: cost, efficacy, safety, durability).

In this document we cover four widely used MCDM methods:

- **TOPSIS**: “closest to the ideal best, farthest from the ideal worst.”
- **VIKOR**: “best compromise,” balancing overall performance and worst-case regret.
- **PROMETHEE**: “who beats whom?” using pairwise comparisons and preference thresholds.
- **ELECTRE**: “outranking with veto,” where a very bad performance on one criterion can block an alternative.

We will use the same simple example for all four methods so the differences are easier to see.

## A shared running example

We want to rank three alternatives: $A$, $B$, $C$.

We evaluate them on three criteria:

- $c_1$ = **Cost** (lower is better) with weight $w_1 = 0.40$
- $c_2$ = **Quality** (higher is better) with weight $w_2 = 0.35$
- $c_3$ = **Delivery time (days)** (lower is better) with weight $w_3 = 0.25$

The data matrix $X = (x_{ij})$ is:

| Alternative | Cost | Quality | Days |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| $A$ | 250 | 7 | 5 |
| $B$ | 200 | 6 | 7 |
| $C$ | 300 | 9 | 4 |

The weights sum to $1$: $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

## TOPSIS

### What question does TOPSIS answer?

“Which option is most similar to an **ideal best** option (best on every criterion) and most dissimilar to an **ideal worst** option (worst on every criterion)?”

### What does TOPSIS embody?

A *distance-to-ideal* idea:

- You imagine a “perfect” alternative (ideal best) and a “terrible” alternative (ideal worst).
- You score each real alternative by how close it is to the perfect one and how far it is from the terrible one.

This is a **compensatory** logic: being very good on one criterion can offset being worse on another.

### Input and output

**Input**

- Alternatives $i=1,\dots,K$
- Criteria $j=1,\dots,M$
- Data $x_{ij}$
- Weights $w_j$
- Direction of each criterion (benefit vs cost)

**Output**

- A ranking using the **closeness coefficient** $C_i$ (higher is better)

### Method with math notation

Step 1: normalize each column (vector normalization):

$$
r_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^K x_{ij}^2}}.
$$

Step 2: apply weights:

$$
v_{ij} = w_j \, r_{ij}.
$$

Step 3: define ideal best $v_j^+$ and ideal worst $v_j^-$.

If criterion $j$ is a benefit (higher is better),

$$
v_j^+ = \max_i v_{ij}, \quad v_j^- = \min_i v_{ij}.
$$

If criterion $j$ is a cost (lower is better),

$$
v_j^+ = \min_i v_{ij}, \quad v_j^- = \max_i v_{ij}.
$$

Step 4: distances to ideal best and worst:

$$
S_i^+ = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^M (v_{ij} - v_j^+)^2},
\qquad
S_i^- = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^M (v_{ij} - v_j^-)^2}.
$$

Step 5: closeness coefficient:

$$
C_i = \frac{S_i^-}{S_i^+ + S_i^-}.
$$

Higher $C_i$ means “closer to ideal best and farther from ideal worst.”

### Example calculation (short but concrete)

Using the running example with weights $(0.40, 0.35, 0.25)$ and treating Cost and Days as “cost” criteria, TOPSIS yields (after normalization and weighting):

- $C_A \approx 0.497$
- $C_B \approx 0.445$
- $C_C \approx 0.555$

So the TOPSIS ranking is:

$$
C \;>\; A \;>\; B.
$$

### Limitations of TOPSIS

- **Sensitive to normalization and scale.** Different normalization choices can change ranks.
- **Fully compensatory.** An alternative can be very bad on one criterion but still rank high if it is very good on others.
- **Weights do a lot of work.** If weights are uncertain, rankings can be unstable.

### Which of the other methods can address these limitations?

- If you want to reduce “one-criterion disaster” being hidden, **ELECTRE** (via veto) can help.
- If you want explicit “balance overall vs worst-case” control, **VIKOR** can help.
- If you want to treat tiny differences as practically irrelevant (and encode that), **PROMETHEE** can help.

## VIKOR

### What question does VIKOR answer?

“What is the best **compromise** option, balancing overall performance against the risk of being very poor on one criterion?”

### What does VIKOR embody?

Two ideas at once:

- **Group utility**: do well overall across criteria.
- **Individual regret**: avoid being the worst on an important criterion.

VIKOR explicitly computes both and then mixes them.

### Input and output

**Input**

- Same basic inputs as TOPSIS: $x_{ij}$, $w_j$, criterion directions
- A tuning parameter $v \in [0,1]$ that controls the trade-off:
  - larger $v$ emphasizes “overall good”
  - smaller $v$ emphasizes “avoid worst-case regret”

**Output**

- A ranking by $Q_i$ (lower is better), plus (in some versions) a “compromise solution” recommendation based on additional conditions.

### Method with math notation

First define best and worst for each criterion:

- Benefit criterion: $f_j^* = \max_i f_{ij}$, $f_j^- = \min_i f_{ij}$
- Cost criterion: $f_j^* = \min_i f_{ij}$, $f_j^- = \max_i f_{ij}$

Define the normalized gap-to-best $d_{ij}$:

- Benefit:

$$
d_{ij} = \frac{f_j^* - f_{ij}}{f_j^* - f_j^-}
$$

- Cost:

$$
d_{ij} = \frac{f_{ij} - f_j^*}{f_j^- - f_j^*}.
$$

Then compute:

**Group utility**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^M w_j d_{ij},
$$

**Regret**

$$
R_i = \max_j \{ w_j d_{ij} \}.
$$

Then combine into the VIKOR index:

$$
Q_i
=
v \, \frac{S_i - S^*}{S^- - S^*}
+
(1-v)\, \frac{R_i - R^*}{R^- - R^*},
$$

where $S^*=\min_i S_i$, $S^-=\max_i S_i$, and similarly for $R$.

Lower $Q_i$ is better.

### Example calculation (walk-through)

Compute best/worst per criterion from the table:

- Cost (cost): best $=200$, worst $=300$
- Quality (benefit): best $=9$, worst $=6$
- Days (cost): best $=4$, worst $=7$

Compute $d_{ij}$ and then $S_i$:

For $A$:
- Cost: $d = (250-200)/(300-200)=0.5$
- Quality: $d = (9-7)/(9-6)=2/3$
- Days: $d = (5-4)/(7-4)=1/3$

So

$$
S_A = 0.40(0.5) + 0.35(2/3) + 0.25(1/3) \approx 0.5167.
$$

For $B$:
- Cost: $0$
- Quality: $1$
- Days: $1$

$$
S_B = 0.40(0) + 0.35(1) + 0.25(1) = 0.60.
$$

For $C$:
- Cost: $1$
- Quality: $0$
- Days: $0$

$$
S_C = 0.40(1) + 0.35(0) + 0.25(0) = 0.40.
$$

Now regrets $R_i = \max_j w_j d_{ij}$:

- $R_A = \max(0.20, 0.2333, 0.0833)=0.2333$
- $R_B = \max(0, 0.35, 0.25)=0.35$
- $R_C = \max(0.40, 0, 0)=0.40$

Set $v=0.5$ and compute $Q_i$:

- $Q_C = 0$ (best on both $S$ and $R$ here)
- $Q_A \approx 0.492$
- $Q_B \approx 0.786$

So the VIKOR ranking is:

$$
C \;>\; A \;>\; B.
$$

### Limitations of VIKOR

- You must choose $v$, and different $v$ can change the ranking.
- Like TOPSIS, it depends on how you define/scale the “gap” (normalization).
- It is still broadly compensatory, although the regret term reduces extreme compensation.

### Which of the other methods can address these limitations?

- If you want explicit thresholds for “practically meaningful differences,” **PROMETHEE** helps.
- If you want non-compensatory “veto” logic (e.g., safety cannot be traded away), **ELECTRE** helps.

## PROMETHEE

### What question does PROMETHEE answer?

“When comparing options **pairwise**, which option tends to be preferred over others, taking into account criterion weights and the idea that small differences may not matter?”

### What does PROMETHEE embody?

A pairwise “voting” perspective:

- For each criterion, you compare $a$ to $b$ and convert the difference into a preference degree between $0$ and $1$.
- You combine preferences across criteria using weights.
- You compute how much an alternative outranks others (positive flow) and how much it is outranked (negative flow).

This is very natural when your criteria have noise and you want to say: “a tiny difference is basically nothing.”

### Input and output

**Input**

- $x_{ij}$, weights $w_j$, criterion directions
- A choice of **preference function** $P_j(\Delta)$ for each criterion
- Possibly thresholds:
  - indifference threshold $q$ (differences smaller than $q$ are negligible)
  - preference threshold $p$ (differences larger than $p$ are clearly meaningful)

**Output**

- PROMETHEE II: a complete ranking via net flow $\phi(a)$ (higher is better)

### Method with math notation

For a pair $(a,b)$ define the difference $\Delta_j(a,b)$ in the “good direction.”
For example:

- Benefit: $\Delta_j(a,b) = x_{aj} - x_{bj}$
- Cost: $\Delta_j(a,b) = x_{bj} - x_{aj}$ (so positive means $a$ is better)

Define a preference degree:

$$
P_j(a,b) = P_j\bigl(\Delta_j(a,b)\bigr)\in[0,1].
$$

A simple “usual” preference is:

$$
P_j(a,b) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \Delta_j(a,b) > 0,\\
0, & \Delta_j(a,b) \le 0.
\end{cases}
$$

Aggregate across criteria:

$$
\pi(a,b) = \sum_{j=1}^M w_j P_j(a,b).
$$

Compute flows for each alternative $a$:

$$
\phi^+(a) = \frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{b\ne a}\pi(a,b),
\qquad
\phi^-(a) = \frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{b\ne a}\pi(b,a).
$$

Net flow:

$$
\phi(a)=\phi^+(a)-\phi^-(a).
$$

### Example calculation (with a simple preference rule)

Use the “usual” preference function above: on each criterion, $a$ gets credit $1$ if it beats $b$, else $0$.

Compute $\pi(a,b)$.

- Compare $A$ vs $B$:
  - Cost: $A$ is worse (250 vs 200) $\Rightarrow 0$
  - Quality: $A$ is better (7 vs 6) $\Rightarrow 1$
  - Days: $A$ is better (5 vs 7) $\Rightarrow 1$
  - So $\pi(A,B)=0.35+0.25=0.60$

- Compare $A$ vs $C$:
  - Cost: $A$ is better than $C$ (250 vs 300) $\Rightarrow 1$
  - Quality: $A$ is worse than $C$ (7 vs 9) $\Rightarrow 0$
  - Days: $A$ is worse than $C$ (5 vs 4) $\Rightarrow 0$
  - So $\pi(A,C)=0.40$

- Compare $B$ vs $A$: $\pi(B,A)=0.40$ (B better on cost only)
- Compare $B$ vs $C$: B better on cost only $\Rightarrow \pi(B,C)=0.40$
- Compare $C$ vs $A$: C better on quality and days $\Rightarrow \pi(C,A)=0.35+0.25=0.60$
- Compare $C$ vs $B$: C better on quality and days $\Rightarrow \pi(C,B)=0.60$

Now flows (here $K=3$, so divide by $2$):

$$
\phi^+(A)=\frac{\pi(A,B)+\pi(A,C)}{2}=\frac{0.60+0.40}{2}=0.50,
$$

$$
\phi^-(A)=\frac{\pi(B,A)+\pi(C,A)}{2}=\frac{0.40+0.60}{2}=0.50,
$$

so $\phi(A)=0$.

Similarly:

- $\phi(B)=\frac{0.40+0.40}{2}-\frac{0.60+0.60}{2}=0.40-0.60=-0.20$
- $\phi(C)=\frac{0.60+0.60}{2}-\frac{0.40+0.40}{2}=0.60-0.40=+0.20$

PROMETHEE II ranking:

$$
C \;>\; A \;>\; B.
$$

### Limitations of PROMETHEE

- You must choose preference functions and thresholds; results can vary with these choices.
- It is still a preference aggregation method: it does not automatically enforce hard constraints unless you encode them.
- Pairwise logic can be harder to explain than TOPSIS for some audiences.

### Which of the other methods can address these limitations?

- If you want a simpler “geometric” story, **TOPSIS** is often easier to communicate.
- If you want explicit “veto / must-not-fail” safety rules, **ELECTRE** is the more direct framework.
- If you want an explicit “overall vs worst-case” compromise knob $v$, **VIKOR** gives that.

## ELECTRE

### What question does ELECTRE answer?

“Can we credibly say that option $a$ is **at least as good as** option $b$, considering most criteria support it, and there is **no criterion where $a$ is unacceptably worse** (a veto situation)?”

### What does ELECTRE embody?

A non-compensatory idea:

- It is not enough to be good on average.
- If you are *too bad* on one important criterion, you can be blocked (veto).
- This matches many real decisions: for example, in safety problems you may refuse any option with unacceptable adverse events even if efficacy is high.

### Input and output

**Input**

- $x_{ij}$, weights $w_j$, benefit/cost directions
- A **concordance rule** (how much weighted support is enough)
- A **discordance/veto rule** (how bad is “too bad”)

**Output**

- An **outranking relation** (a directed graph of “$a$ outranks $b$”)
- Depending on ELECTRE variant: a partial order, complete ranking, or assignment into categories.

### Method with math notation (basic ELECTRE-style skeleton)

For each pair $(a,b)$, define the set of criteria where $a$ is at least as good as $b$:

$$
J^+(a,b) = \{ j : a \text{ is at least as good as } b \text{ on criterion } j \}.
$$

The **concordance index** is:

$$
C(a,b) = \sum_{j\in J^+(a,b)} w_j.
$$

Then define a discordance/veto idea:
If there exists any criterion $j$ where $a$ is much worse than $b$ (worse than a veto threshold), then $a$ does **not** outrank $b$ even if concordance is high.

A simple rule is:

- Declare $a$ outranks $b$ if $C(a,b)\ge c^*$ and no veto is triggered.

### Example calculation (simple concordance + veto)

Let’s pick a concordance threshold $c^*=0.65$ and a veto rule:

- Veto on **Days** if $a$ is more than 2 days slower than $b$.
- Veto on **Cost** if $a$ is more than 80 euros more expensive than $b$.

Now compare pairs.

**Does $A$ outrank $B$?**

- Criteria where $A$ is at least as good:
  - Quality: yes (7 ≥ 6) weight 0.35
  - Days: yes (5 ≤ 7) weight 0.25
  - Cost: no (250 is worse than 200)
- Concordance: $C(A,B)=0.35+0.25=0.60$ which is below $0.65$
- So $A$ does not outrank $B$ under this $c^*$.

**Does $C$ outrank $A$?**

- Quality: yes (9 ≥ 7) weight 0.35
- Days: yes (4 ≤ 5) weight 0.25
- Cost: no (300 worse than 250)
- Concordance: $0.60$ (still below $0.65$), so not enough support.

**Does $A$ outrank $C$?**

- Cost: yes (250 ≤ 300) weight 0.40
- Quality: no
- Days: no
- Concordance: $0.40$, not enough.

So with a strict threshold, you may get **no outranking at all**. That is not a bug: ELECTRE can produce a **partial order** when evidence is not strong enough.

If we relax to $c^*=0.60$, then:
- $A$ outranks $B$ (concordance $0.60$ and no veto triggered)
- $C$ outranks $A$ (concordance $0.60$ and no cost veto because $300-250=50 \le 80$)
- $C$ outranks $B$ (quality + days support; no cost veto because $300-200=100$ would trigger cost veto if threshold is 80, so this depends on your veto choice)

This shows why ELECTRE is powerful: you can represent real “deal-breaker” rules, but you must choose thresholds carefully.

### Limitations of ELECTRE

- Many design choices (concordance threshold, discordance/veto thresholds) can feel subjective.
- Can produce partial rankings (incomparability), which may frustrate users who demand a single “winner.”
- More complex to communicate than TOPSIS/VIKOR to beginners.

### Which of the other methods can address these limitations?

- If you need a complete ranking with a simpler story, **TOPSIS** often provides that.
- If you want a complete ranking but still handle “small differences,” **PROMETHEE II** provides a net-flow ranking.
- If you want a compromise ranking with explicit worst-case control but fewer veto-style choices, **VIKOR** may be simpler.

## Summary comparison: what each method is really “doing”

### TOPSIS

- Core idea: distance to “ideal best” and “ideal worst”
- Reflects: smooth trade-offs (compensation) across criteria
- Good when: you accept trade-offs and want an intuitive geometric score

### VIKOR

- Core idea: compromise between overall performance ($S$) and worst-case regret ($R$)
- Reflects: “do well overall but avoid being terrible somewhere”
- Good when: you want a negotiated compromise and can justify a balance parameter $v$

### PROMETHEE

- Core idea: pairwise outranking strength via preference functions and thresholds
- Reflects: practical significance (tiny differences can be ignored)
- Good when: data are noisy and you want “preference with thresholds”

### ELECTRE

- Core idea: outranking with concordance + discordance/veto (non-compensatory)
- Reflects: “deal-breakers” and safety constraints
- Good when: one bad criterion should block an option (e.g., safety)

## Practical advice for beginners

- If you need a simple, explainable ranking and you accept trade-offs: start with **TOPSIS**.
- If you want “overall good” but also to protect against the worst-case: try **VIKOR**.
- If you want to treat small differences as negligible and compare options pairwise: use **PROMETHEE**.
- If you have hard constraints or veto-like rules (especially safety): consider **ELECTRE**.

## A note on “weights” and uncertainty

All four methods usually require weights $w_j$. In real problems, weights are rarely known perfectly.

A common statistical habit is to do a **sensitivity analysis**:
- vary weights within reasonable ranges,
- re-rank alternatives,
- and report how stable the conclusion is.

This is often more honest than presenting one ranking as “the truth.”

## Recommendations for choosing a method

Choosing an MCDM method is not about finding “the best method in general.”  
It is about matching the method to the *decision philosophy* you actually want.

Below is the checklist I recommend you follow.

### Step 1: Write down the decision goal in plain words

Before touching formulas, write one sentence:

- “I need a **single ranked list** of options.”
- “I only need to **screen out unacceptable** options, then rank the rest.”
- “I need a **compromise choice** that is defensible to multiple stakeholders.”
- “I must enforce at least one **deal-breaker** (safety/feasibility constraint).”

If you cannot write this sentence, you are not ready to pick a method.

### Step 2: Decide whether you allow trade-offs (compensation)

Ask:

- “Is it acceptable that being excellent on one criterion can compensate for being poor on another?”

If **yes**, you are in a **compensatory** mindset:
- TOPSIS and VIKOR are natural starting points.

If **no**, you are in a **non-compensatory** mindset:
- ELECTRE is often the best match, because it can implement veto/deal-breaker logic.

PROMETHEE can be either, depending on how you design preference functions and thresholds, but by default it is closer to “soft trade-offs.”

### Step 3: Ask whether *small differences should be ignored*

In real data, tiny differences may be noise, measurement error, or clinically negligible.

Ask:

- “If two options differ only slightly, do I want to treat them as essentially equal?”

If **yes**, favor methods that explicitly encode this:

- PROMETHEE (with indifference threshold $q$ and preference threshold $p$)
- ELECTRE (with indifference/preference/veto thresholds in ELECTRE-III style)

If **no**, and you want all differences to count smoothly:
- TOPSIS or VIKOR are fine.

### Step 4: Ask whether you have “must-not-fail” criteria

These are criteria where being too bad is unacceptable, even if everything else looks good.

Examples:
- toxicity/adverse events beyond a threshold,
- manufacturing feasibility,
- minimum durability requirement,
- maximum cost cap.

If you have “must-not-fail” criteria, then you should not rely on a purely compensatory method alone.

Recommendation:

- Use ELECTRE (veto thresholds), or
- do a **hard screening step first** (remove infeasible options), then apply TOPSIS/VIKOR/PROMETHEE to the remaining set.

### Step 5: Decide if you want an explicit “balance knob” between average and worst-case

If stakeholders argue between:

- “best overall average performance” versus
- “avoid being terrible on any one important criterion”

then VIKOR is attractive because it makes this explicit with $v$:

- large $v$ emphasizes overall performance ($S$),
- small $v$ emphasizes worst-case regret ($R$).

If your stakeholders do not want to discuss such a knob, TOPSIS may be easier.

### Step 6: My practical “default choices” for students

Here is what I recommend as a default workflow.

#### Case A: You want a simple, explainable full ranking and accept trade-offs

Start with **TOPSIS**.

Why:
- very interpretable story (ideal best/worst),
- gives a single score $C_i$,
- easy to communicate.

Then do a sensitivity analysis on weights and normalization.

#### Case B: You want a compromise solution and worry about “one really bad criterion”

Start with **VIKOR** (use $v=0.5$ as a default, then test $v$ values).

Why:
- separates “overall performance” and “worst-case regret,”
- gives you a compromise ranking $Q_i$.

#### Case C: Your data are noisy or you want “practically meaningful” comparisons

Start with **PROMETHEE II**.

Why:
- preference functions let you encode “small differences don’t matter,”
- produces a net-flow ranking $\phi(a)$,
- intuitive in pairwise terms (“who tends to beat whom?”).

#### Case D: You have veto constraints (safety/feasibility deal-breakers)

Start with **ELECTRE**.

Why:
- concordance + veto logic matches “must-not-fail” decisions,
- can represent real policy constraints.

Be aware you may get partial rankings; that may actually be the honest answer.

### Step 7: What I want you to report (even in a homework)

No matter which method you choose, I want these three things reported.

#### (1) Your preference assumptions

State clearly:

- which criteria are benefit vs cost,
- weights $w_j$ and how you chose them,
- any thresholds ($q, p,$ veto) and why they are reasonable.

#### (2) A stability check (sensitivity analysis)

At minimum:

- vary weights by $\pm 10\%$ (then renormalize) and re-rank,
- report how often the top choice changes.

If the top choice changes frequently, your conclusion is not stable.

#### (3) A “sanity check” using a second method

I strongly recommend:

- Use one method as your primary ranking,
- and confirm the top group using another method with a different philosophy.

Examples:
- TOPSIS primary + ELECTRE veto check,
- VIKOR primary + PROMETHEE with thresholds as robustness,
- PROMETHEE primary + TOPSIS as a smooth-distance comparison.

If both methods agree on the top alternative(s), you have a more defensible decision.

### Step 8: Common beginner mistakes (and how to avoid them)

- **Mistake:** picking a method before stating decision philosophy  
  **Fix:** write the “plain words” goal first.

- **Mistake:** using weights as if they are known facts  
  **Fix:** do sensitivity analysis and report uncertainty.

- **Mistake:** forgetting that veto constraints are not “preferences”  
  **Fix:** enforce feasibility first or use ELECTRE-style veto rules.

- **Mistake:** believing one ranking is “the truth”  
  **Fix:** treat rankings as a structured summary of assumptions.

### Bottom line

If you are unsure, here is my recommended default sequence:

1. Screen infeasible options (if any hard constraints exist).  
2. Rank with TOPSIS (simple baseline).  
3. Re-rank with VIKOR or PROMETHEE (depending on whether worst-case regret or thresholds matter).  
4. If safety/feasibility is critical, confirm with ELECTRE veto logic.  
5. Report sensitivity to weights and thresholds.

This workflow is not about complexity; it is about being honest and defensible.

## Doing MCDM on the posterior (Bayesian ranking with uncertainty)

In our vaccine setting, we have a small number of regimens (say $K=6$).  
Each regimen is evaluated on two immunogenicity outcomes (say $M=2$), which often creates a trade-off surface in outcome space.

A Bayesian model does not give a single “true” value for each regimen.  
Instead, it gives a **posterior distribution** for the regimen’s true (latent) mean outcomes.  
This is exactly what we want: it allows the final ranking to reflect uncertainty.

### What is the goal?

A standard MCDM method (TOPSIS, VIKOR, PROMETHEE, ELECTRE) expects a *fixed* table of criterion values and returns a ranking.

In a Bayesian analysis, we want a ranking that answers questions like:

- “Which regimen is most likely to be top-ranked?”
- “Which regimens are essentially tied?”
- “How sensitive is the ranking to decision weights or thresholds?”

So we apply MCDM **to each posterior draw**, which converts a single ranking into a **posterior distribution of rankings**.

## Bayesian setup: what are the inputs?

### Alternatives and criteria

- Alternatives (regimens): $k \in \{1,\dots,K\}$, with $K=6$.
- Criteria (immunogenicity outcomes): $j \in \{1,\dots,M\}$, with $M=2$.

### Latent regimen means under a Bayesian model

Let the (unknown) true mean outcomes for regimen $k$ be

$$
\boldsymbol\mu_k = (\mu_{k1}, \mu_{k2}).
$$

After fitting a Bayesian model, we have posterior draws:

$$
\boldsymbol\mu_k^{(s)} = (\mu_{k1}^{(s)}, \mu_{k2}^{(s)}), 
\qquad s=1,\dots,S.
$$

These draws can come from a hierarchical model, a multivariate model, a GP model, etc.  
The MCDM step does not depend on the specific Bayesian model, only on having posterior draws of the regimen-level outcomes.

### Construct the posterior draw matrix

For each posterior draw $s$, define the $K \times M$ matrix

$$
X^{(s)} = \bigl(x_{kj}^{(s)}\bigr), \quad x_{kj}^{(s)} = \mu_{kj}^{(s)}.
$$

This is the “data table” that will be passed into an MCDM method at draw $s$.

## The general posterior-MCDM algorithm

For each draw $s=1,\dots,S$:

1. Build $X^{(s)}$ from posterior draws of regimen outcomes.
2. Apply your chosen MCDM method to $X^{(s)}$.
3. Record the method’s **score** (and implied ranking) for each regimen.

This produces a draw-by-draw score:

$$
T_k^{(s)} = \text{MCDMscore}(X^{(s)}, k),
$$

where $T_k^{(s)}$ could be:

- TOPSIS closeness: $C_k^{(s)}$ (higher is better)
- VIKOR index: $Q_k^{(s)}$ (lower is better)
- PROMETHEE net flow: $\phi_k^{(s)}$ (higher is better)
- ELECTRE outranking indicators (pairwise) $O_{k\ell}^{(s)} \in \{0,1\}$

Then summarize across $s$ to obtain posterior uncertainty on scores and ranks.

## What should you report? (posterior summaries)

### Posterior distribution of the MCDM score

For a score-based method (TOPSIS / VIKOR / PROMETHEE), report for each regimen $k$:

- Posterior mean score $E[T_k \mid \text{data}]$
- Credible interval for $T_k$
- (Optional) posterior probability that $T_k$ exceeds a benchmark

This tells you whether score differences are large or mostly noise.

### Posterior distribution of the rank

Define the rank of regimen $k$ at draw $s$ as:

$$
R_k^{(s)} = \text{rank of } T_k^{(s)} \text{ among } \{T_1^{(s)},\dots,T_K^{(s)}\}.
$$

Then compute:

- Probability regimen $k$ is best:

$$
P(R_k = 1 \mid \text{data})
\approx
\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S \mathbb{1}\{R_k^{(s)}=1\}.
$$

- Probability regimen $k$ is in the top $m$:

$$
P(R_k \le m \mid \text{data})
\approx
\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S \mathbb{1}\{R_k^{(s)}\le m\}.
$$

These are often more interpretable than a single point estimate ranking.

### Pairwise “beats” probabilities

For any two regimens $k$ and $\ell$, define

$$
P(k \text{ beats } \ell \mid \text{data})
\approx
\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S \mathbb{1}\{T_k^{(s)} > T_\ell^{(s)}\},
$$

for methods where “higher score is better.”  
(For VIKOR, replace $>$ by $<$ because lower $Q$ is better.)

This creates a probabilistic pairwise comparison table, which is useful when ranks are uncertain.

## Method-specific notes

### TOPSIS on the posterior

For each draw $s$:

- compute $C_k^{(s)}$ from $X^{(s)}$
- rank by larger $C_k^{(s)}$

Posterior interpretation:

- “Regimen $k$ is closest to the ideal solution with probability $P(R_k=1 \mid \text{data})$.”

What it embodies under uncertainty:

- a smooth trade-off rule applied repeatedly across plausible “true values” of outcomes.

### VIKOR on the posterior

For each draw $s$:

- compute $S_k^{(s)}$ (overall shortfall)
- compute $R_k^{(s)}$ (worst-case shortfall)
- compute $Q_k^{(s)}$ using a chosen $v$
- rank by smaller $Q_k^{(s)}$

Posterior interpretation:

- “Regimen $k$ is the best compromise with probability $P(R_k=1 \mid \text{data})$.”

Important practical step:

- do sensitivity analysis over $v$ (for example $v \in \{0.25,0.5,0.75\}$), because rankings can change with $v$.

### PROMETHEE on the posterior

For each draw $s$, compute:

- pairwise preferences $\pi^{(s)}(k,\ell)$ using preference functions and thresholds
- net flow $\phi_k^{(s)}$
- rank by larger $\phi_k^{(s)}$

Posterior interpretation:

- “Regimen $k$ tends to outrank others, after ignoring negligible differences.”

Why it pairs well with Bayesian models:

- the preference thresholds ($q$ and $p$) can be aligned with what is clinically negligible, while posterior draws represent statistical uncertainty.

### ELECTRE on the posterior

ELECTRE is naturally pairwise and can include veto logic.

For each draw $s$ and each pair $(k,\ell)$ define an outranking indicator:

$$
O_{k\ell}^{(s)} =
\begin{cases}
1, & k \text{ outranks } \ell \text{ at draw } s,\\
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$

Then estimate the posterior outranking probability:

$$
P(k \succ \ell \mid \text{data})
\approx
\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S O_{k\ell}^{(s)}.
$$

This produces a probabilistic outranking graph rather than a single deterministic one.

## A Bayesian screening step before ranking (strongly recommended)

With $M=2$ immunogenicity outcomes, it is often sensible to separate the workflow into:

1. **Screening**: identify regimens that are plausibly nondominated.
2. **Ranking**: apply MCDM to the plausible set.

### Posterior probability of being nondominated

Let regimen $k$ be nondominated at draw $s$ if no other regimen is better on both outcomes at that draw.  
Define indicator:

$$
N_k^{(s)} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & k \text{ is nondominated at draw } s,\\
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$

Then:

$$
P(k \text{ is nondominated} \mid \text{data})
\approx
\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S N_k^{(s)}.
$$

A practical rule is to rank only regimens with high nondominance probability (for example above $0.5$), and treat the rest as unlikely winners.

## When does posterior-MCDM become questionable?

Posterior-MCDM is technically feasible, but it can become scientifically unhelpful if:

- the decision weights or thresholds cannot be justified,
- both outcomes are so uncertain that ranks are unstable,
- or “higher is better” is not a valid monotone preference across the outcome range.

In those cases, the best output is often:

- a set of plausible top regimens with posterior probabilities,
- not a forced single winner.

## What I recommend you show in your final report

For each regimen:

- posterior mean and credible interval of each immunogenicity outcome
- $P(\text{nondominated} \mid \text{data})$
- posterior rank distribution under the chosen MCDM method
- sensitivity analysis:
  - weights (for all methods)
  - and thresholds or $v$ (for PROMETHEE/ELECTRE/VIKOR)

This makes the decision rule transparent and defensible.
